Babylon was a flat country, abundantly watered. The destruction of Babylon, so often prophesied by Isaiah, was typical of the destruction of the great foe of the New Testament church, as foretold in Revelation. It would be welcome news to the poor oppressed captives, but grievous to the proud oppressors. Let this check vain mirth and sensual pleasures, because we do not know when the mirth may end. Here is the alarm given to Babylon, when forced by Cyrus. An ass and a camel seem to be symbols of the Medes and Persians. Babylon's idols will be so far from protecting her that they will be broken down. True believers are the corn of God’s floor; hypocrites are but as chaff and straw, with which the wheat is now mixed, but from which it shall be separated. The corn of God’s floor must expect to be threshed by afflictions and persecutions. God's Israel of old was afflicted. Even then God says that it is still his. In all events concerning the church, past, present, and future, we must look to God, who has power to do anything for his church, and grace to do everything that is for her good.

God’s prophets and ministers are like watchmen in the city in a time of peace, to see that all is safe. They are like watchmen in the camp in time of war, to warn of the enemy's movements. After a long sleep in sin and security, it is time to rise and awake. We have much work to do; we have a long journey to go; it is time to be stirring. After a long dark night, is there any hope of the day dawning? What news of the night? What will happen tonight? We must never be secure. But many make curious inquiries of the watchmen. They would willingly have difficult questions solved or prophecies interpreted, but they do not seek into the state of their own souls, about the way of salvation and the path of duty. The watchman answers by way of prophecy. There comes first a morning of light, peace, and opportunity; but afterward comes a night of trouble and calamity. If there is a morning of youth and health, old age and sickness will come; if there is a morning of prosperity in the family or publicly, we must look for changes. It is our wisdom to improve the present morning in preparation for the night that is coming after it. Inquire, return, come. We are urged to do it quickly, because there is no time to trifle. Those who return and come to God will find they have much work to do, and little time to do it.

The Arabians lived in tents and kept cattle. A destroying army will be brought upon them and make them an easy prey. We do not know what difficulties we may face before we die. Those who now eat bread to the full may know the want of necessary food. Neither the skill of archers nor the courage of mighty men can protect from the judgments of God. That is poor glory which will quickly come to nothing. Thus the Lord said to me, and no word of his will fail. We may be sure that the Strength of Israel will not lie. Happy are those only whose riches and glory are out of the reach of invaders; all other prosperity will speedily pass away.
